Question: The mass and charge of a water droplet are $$1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{g}$$ and $$2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{C} ,$$ respectively. If the droplet is given an initial horizontal velocity of $$5.0 \times 10^{5} \mathbf{\hat{i}} \text{m/s} ,$$ what magnetic field will keep it moving in this direction? Why must gravity be considered here?
Answer: $$9.8 \times 10^{-5} \mathbf{\hat{j}} \text{T} ;$$ the magnetic and gravitational forces must balance to maintain dynamic equilibrium

Charge of the drop  
q = 2.0 × 10⁻⁸ C  

Initial velocity  
v⃗ = 5.0 × 10⁵  î  m s⁻¹      (purely +x direction)

To keep the drop moving in this same horizontal direction its resultant force must be zero.  
The two forces that can act are

1. the weight  W⃗ = m g (−ĵ)  
2. the magnetic force  F⃗_B = q( v⃗ × B⃗ )

We therefore require  

q( v⃗ × B⃗ ) + m g (−ĵ) = 0.

Choose the magnetic field so that v⃗ × B⃗ points upward (+ĵ) and equals the weight in magnitude.

Direction of B⃗  
v⃗ is along +î.  For v⃗ × B⃗ to be +ĵ we need B⃗ along −k̂ (into the page), because  

î × (−k̂) = +ĵ.

Magnitude of B⃗  
|F_B| = q v B = m g

⇒ B = m g / (q v)

   = (1.0 × 10⁻⁷ kg)(9.8 m s⁻²) / (2.0 × 10⁻⁸ C)(5.0 × 10⁵ m s⁻¹)

   = 9.8 × 10⁻⁷ / 1.0 × 10⁻²  

   ≈ 9.8 × 10⁻⁵ T ≃ 1 × 10⁻⁴ T.

Result  

B⃗ ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁴ T  (directed along −k̂, i.e. into the page).

Why gravity must be considered  
Without balancing the gravitational force, the massive water droplet would accelerate downward and its trajectory would curve; a magnetic field alone cannot supply an upward component unless it is expressly chosen to do so. To keep the motion strictly horizontal, the magnetic force must cancel the weight, so gravity has to be included in the force balance.

Step 1. Electric field between the plates  
V = 300 V,   d = 1.0 cm = 0.010 m  
E = V / d = 300 V / 0.010 m = 3.0 × 10⁴ V m⁻¹

Step 2. Speed of the 2000 eV electron  
Kinetic energy: KE = 2000 eV = 2000(1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J) = 3.204 × 10⁻¹⁶ J  

Using KE = ½ mv² (non-relativistic is adequate: v ≈ 0.09 c)  

v = √(2 KE / m)  
  = √[2(3.204 × 10⁻¹⁶ J) / 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg]  
  ≈ √(7.04 × 10¹⁴)  
  ≈ 2.65 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹

B = E / v = (3.0 × 10⁴ V m⁻¹) / (2.65 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹)

Question: Consider an electron rotating in a circular orbit of radius r. Show that the magnitudes of the magnetic dipole moment μ and the angular momentum L of the electron are related by: $$\frac{\mu}{L} = \frac{e}{2 m} .$$

 T = 2π/ω.  

Hence the magnitude of the current is  

 I = |q| / T = e / (2π/ω) = eω / 2π.

2. Magnetic-dipole moment of the orbit  

The magnetic-dipole moment produced by a planar current loop is  

 μ⃗ = I A n̂, A = πr².  

Its magnitude is therefore  

 |μ| = I πr² = (eω/2π) πr² = eωr² / 2.

3. Orbital angular momentum  

For uniform circular motion the magnitude of the orbital angular momentum is  

 L = m v r = m (ωr) r = m ω r².

4. Ratio μ / L  

 μ / L = (eωr² / 2) / (m ω r²) = e / (2m).

Taking only magnitudes removes the minus sign associated with the electron’s negative charge, so we obtain  

 |μ| / |L| = e / 2m.

Hence the magnetic dipole moment and the orbital angular momentum of the electron are related by  

 μ / L = e / 2m.

The angular momentum of a point particle of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ at a radius $r$ from the center of rotation is given by the magnitude:

$$ \boxed{\frac{\mu}{L} = \frac{e}{2m}} $$
